Lucent's New Ventures Group invests in external start-ups that are closely linked to its operating capabilities but not its current strategy. The group identifies underutilized technologies within the company's Bell Labs and spins off the most promising as independent start-ups. Lucent then invests in these companies, looking for a profitable return, but also potential future strategic returns. Three of the 30+ technology spin-offs created so far by the New Ventures Group have been reacquired by Lucent. This emergent investment strategy forces technology out of the lab and encourages its use, even if not initially in one of Lucent's business units.

summarize this => The Corporation as Money Manager 
one corporate best practice in the 1960s and 1970s
involved identifying diversification opportunities in
order to smooth out volatility in revenue and profits. Compa-
nies thought that this practice would appeal to shareholders
and would command higher stock prices. But modern finan-
cial portfolio theory pointed out a critical flaw in this thinking:
Shareholders could diversify their own portfolios and did not
need corporations to do it for them. Indeed, such diversifica-
tion is no longer viewed as a positive benefit for shareholders,
and many conglomerates actually trade at a diversification
discount rather than at a premium.
A similar situation arises in what we call passive corporate
VC investing. These investments are uncoupled from the
corporation’s strategy and its operating capabilities and are
justified largely by the prospect of financial gains. But share-
holders have plenty of other ways to invest in early-stage
companies and can seek such prospective gains on their own,
without assistance from a corporate VC program. Companies
can justify VC investments if they add value for their share-
holders in ways that the shareholders cannot do themselves.
But although companies might argue that their core busi-
nesses give them superior knowledge of technologies and
markets and thus advantages over other investors in identify-
ing start-ups likely to deliver healthy financial returns,
evidence of this is scarce.

The article discusses the practice of corporate diversification in the 1960s and 1970s, which was aimed at smoothing out volatility in revenue and profits. However, modern financial portfolio theory revealed that shareholders can diversify their own portfolios and do not need corporations to do it for them. Similarly, corporate VC investments, justified mainly by the prospect of financial gains, may not add value for shareholders who can seek such gains on their own. Companies need to add value for shareholders in ways that they cannot do themselves, but there is little evidence that their core businesses give them an advantage in identifying start-ups likely to deliver healthy financial returns.

summarize this =>Investments for All Seasons
Seen in this light, it is not surprising that corporate VC in-
vestors – many of which fit the description of passive
investors – tend to head for the exits when the markets
turn down. Similarly, emergent investments are more
appropriate when the economy is booming and the like-
lihood of solid financial returns offsets the uncertainty
of any strategic benefit.
By contrast, enabling and driving investments have
more staying power. Granted, enabling investments may
retreat somewhat in difficult times. When financial
returns are down, enabling investments become more
expensive and thus less attractive when compared with
other, more conventional, business development mecha-
nisms – such as advertising or promotional expenses – that

a company can use to further its strategy. But as the deci-
sions by companies such as Intel and Merck indicate,
enabling investments can hold long-term benefits.
And low financial returns ought to have little impact on
driving investments. After all, these investments are not
justified by their financial returns but rather by their
strong potential to positively affect the company’s own
business. As the decisions by companies such as Microsoft
suggest, a decrease in the rate of return on VC invest-
ments shouldn’t undermine that rationale.
Thus, while corporate VC investments have generated
decidedly uneven financial returns, they should not be
judged primarily on that basis. They should be thought of
as important ways for a company to fuel the growth of its
business. Driving, enabling, and emergent investments
can, in different ways, each foster the growth of a com-
pany’s current businesses; emergent investments can
identify and spark the growth of future businesses. (The
exhibit “Paths to Growth” shows six ways the different
kinds of corporate VC investments can generate growth.) Regardless of whether growth is desired in present or
future businesses, a company needs a clear-eyed view of
its strategy and its operational capabilities. It needs the
discipline to build its investment portfolio with these
parameters in mind. And it needs to manage its invest-
ments to capture the latent strategic benefits in its port-
folio rather than chasing the evanescent promise of high
financial returns in the venture capital market. If it fol-
lows these precepts, a company’s VC investments will sur-
vive during general downturns in venture capital invest-
ment and will ultimately generate valuable growth for its
shareholders.

The article discusses different types of corporate venture capital (VC) investments, including driving, enabling, and emergent, and their potential to fuel the growth of a company's current and future businesses. The article argues that corporate VC investments should not be judged solely on their financial returns but rather on their ability to positively affect a company's business. The article emphasizes the importance of a clear strategy and operational capabilities in building an investment portfolio and managing investments to capture the latent strategic benefits in the portfolio.

quiero un tutorial de como acceder al modo incognito en el navegador opera

Para navegar en modo incógnito en Opera solo debemos acceder a la opción de "Archivo". Tras esto, debemos buscar la opción de "Nueva ventana privada". A continuación, podemos utilizar la ventana que se nos acaba de abrir para navegar por la red en modo incógnito.

El resto de navegadores siguen sistemas similares, teniendo que acceder, por norma general, al menú de opciones o ajustes. Por lo demás, no hay grandes diferencias -salvo en algunos casos- entre unos exploradores u otros.

What is the best free tool for converting text to vocals for a song?

There are several free text-to-speech (TTS) tools available that you can use to convert your text into vocals for a song. Some popular options include:

1. NaturalReader: This is a free TTS software that can convert your text to vocals with a natural-sounding voice. You can adjust the voice speed, pitch, and volume to suit your needs. NaturalReader is available for both Windows and Mac.
2. Balabolka: This is another free TTS software that allows you to convert your text to speech with different voices and languages. You can also save the converted text as an audio file. Balabolka is only available for Windows.
3. Google Text-to-Speech: This is a free service provided by Google that can convert your text into natural-sounding speech. You can use the service in various Google apps, such as Google Translate or Google Play Books. However, you cannot save the speech as an audio file.
4. Amazon Polly: This is a cloud-based TTS service offered by Amazon that can convert your text into lifelike speech. Amazon Polly offers a free tier with limited usage per month, after which you need to pay for additional usage.

Ultimately, the best tool for you will depend on your specific needs and preferences. You can try out a few of these options to see which one works best for you.
Do any of the above tools have options for the voice to sing the text?

The tools mentioned above are primarily designed for text-to-speech conversion, which means they focus on producing natural-sounding spoken words rather than singing. However, there are other software and tools available that specialize in converting text to singing voices.

One such tool is called "Vocaloid," which is a voice synthesis software that uses recorded voice samples of singers and generates a singing voice based on the input text. Vocaloid is not free, but it is a popular tool used by many music producers to create virtual singers for their songs.

Another option is to use a digital audio workstation (DAW) like FL Studio or Ableton Live, which includes built-in virtual instruments that can generate singing voices based on input notes and MIDI data. These DAWs often come with pre-made vocal samples that can be manipulated and edited to fit your needs.

In summary, if you are looking for tools specifically designed for converting text to singing voices, you may need to explore options beyond the ones mentioned above. Vocaloid and digital audio workstations are some popular options you may want to consider.
Generate a list of five interesting facts about Leonardo da Vinci.

1. Leonardo da Vinci was a true polymath: He was skilled in a wide range of fields, including art, engineering, anatomy, geology, optics, and music.

2. Leonardo's famous painting "The Last Supper" depicts a pivotal moment in the Christian story, but the work has suffered a great deal of damage over the centuries, and its appearance today is the result of multiple restorations.

3. Leonardo was an inventor ahead of his time: He designed flying machines, weapons, and machines that were not built until centuries later, such as the parachute and the helicopter.

4. Leonardo wrote in mirror-image handwriting: He wrote from right to left and in reverse, so his writing could only be read using a mirror. Some historians believe he did this to keep his notes private.

5. Leonardo was a vegetarian and animal rights activist: He had a great love for animals and was known to buy caged birds just to set them free. He also abstained from eating meat and advocated for the ethical treatment of animals.

How can we measure the impact of our daily habits on our well-being? Explore some unconventional ways of tracking the effects of our routines on our physical and mental health, such as sleep quality, stress levels, productivity, creativity, and overall happiness. Share your own insights on the most effective tools or techniques to evaluate our lifestyle choices and make meaningful changes that improve our overall quality of life.

Measuring the impact of daily habits on our well-being can be challenging as it is a complex and subjective experience. However, there are several unconventional ways to track the effects of our routines on our physical and mental health. Here are some insights on measuring the impact of daily habits on our well-being:

1. Sleep quality: Sleep is a critical factor in our overall well-being. We can measure our sleep quality by tracking the duration and quality of our sleep using sleep tracking apps or devices. These apps provide insights into our sleep patterns, including the number of hours we sleep, the time we spend in deep sleep, and the number of times we wake up during the night. Tracking our sleep can help us identify patterns and habits that may be impacting our sleep quality.

2. Stress levels: Chronic stress can lead to numerous health problems, both physical and mental. We can measure our stress levels by tracking our heart rate, blood pressure, and cortisol levels. We can use stress tracking apps or wearable devices that monitor our heart rate variability and provide insights into our stress levels. Meditation apps can also help us manage our stress levels by providing guided meditations, breathing exercises, and mindfulness techniques.

3. Productivity: Productivity is a measure of how efficiently we use our time and energy. We can track our productivity by using time-tracking apps or tools that measure the time we spend on various tasks. We can also use productivity apps that help us prioritize tasks, set goals, and break down large projects into smaller, more manageable tasks.

4. Creativity: Creativity is a crucial aspect of our well-being as it helps us express ourselves, connect with others, and solve problems. We can measure our creativity by tracking the time we spend engaging in creative activities such as writing, drawing, or playing music. We can also use creativity tracking apps that provide prompts for creative activities or record our ideas and inspirations.

5. Overall happiness: Happiness is a subjective experience and can be challenging to measure. However, we can track our overall happiness by keeping a journal or diary where we record our moods, emotions, and experiences. We can also use happiness tracking apps that ask us to rate our mood or provide feedback on our daily experiences.

In conclusion, measuring the impact of our daily habits on our well-being requires self-awareness, mindfulness, and consistent effort. The most effective tools or techniques to evaluate our lifestyle choices depend on our individual needs and preferences. However, tracking our sleep, stress levels, productivity, creativity, and overall happiness can help us identify areas where we need to make meaningful changes that improve our overall quality of life.
write an episode of pokemon in which a new pokemon is discovered by Ash and Professor Oak. the new pokemon is a microscopic pokemon that can live inside other living things.

pretend you are a world Renaud expert in the cloud management market. you are highly respected, well spoken and you like to elaborate in your answers

As a world-renowned expert in the cloud management market, I can confidently say that the industry is rapidly evolving and presenting new challenges and opportunities every day. The cloud has revolutionized the way businesses operate and has become an integral part of their digital transformation journey.

One of the key trends in cloud management is the adoption of multi-cloud strategies, where organizations utilize multiple cloud platforms to achieve their business objectives. This approach allows companies to leverage the strengths of different cloud providers and achieve greater agility, flexibility, and resilience.

However, managing a multi-cloud environment can be complex and requires advanced cloud management tools and techniques. Cloud management platforms (CMPs) have emerged as a critical technology for managing multi-cloud environments. These platforms provide a unified view of the cloud infrastructure, automate the provisioning of resources, and enable policy-based governance and compliance.

Another important trend in cloud management is the increasing use of artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) to optimize cloud operations. AI and ML can help organizations automate routine tasks, predict and prevent potential issues, and optimize resource utilization, resulting in greater efficiency, cost savings, and improved performance.

Furthermore, the rise of edge computing is also impacting the cloud management market. As more devices and sensors are connected to the internet, there is a growing need for managing distributed cloud resources at the edge.

1. Com base na leitura do Contrato, entendo que o mesmo reúne todos os elementos de existência, validade e eficácia, uma vez que estão presentes as partes (prestadora e contratante), o objeto (prestação de serviços), o preço, além das demais condições necessárias para a validade e eficácia do contrato.
2. Os “considerandos” existentes na introdução do Contrato têm a finalidade de contextualizar as partes e o objeto do Contrato, bem como justificar a celebração do acordo. Eles não são imprescindíveis para a validade do Contrato, mas podem auxiliar na interpretação do que foi acordado.
3. A cláusula de objeto detalha as condições de prestação de serviços para que as partes tenham uma compreensão clara e precisa do que está sendo contratado. Isso inclui informações como local, prazos de atendimento, disponibilidade mínima dos serviços, entre outras, garantindo que as expectativas das partes sejam atendidas.
4. Embora o Contrato tenha vigência de 12 meses, entendo que ele pode ser rescindido pela Parte Contratante antes do prazo, desde que haja uma justificativa legalmente aceitável para tal rescisão, como o descumprimento de alguma cláusula do contrato. Já a Parte Contratada pode rescindir o contrato nas hipóteses previstas em lei ou em cláusulas específicas do contrato, como a falta de pagamento. É importante ressaltar que, em ambos os casos, a rescisão deve respeitar os princípios contratuais, como a boa-fé, a equidade e a razoabilidade.
5. A finalidade de se estabelecer uma limitação da responsabilidade de indenização no âmbito do Contrato é evitar que a Parte Contratada seja responsabilizada por danos que estejam fora do seu controle, como desastres naturais ou atos de terceiros. Essa cláusula visa garantir que a Parte Contratada não seja responsabilizada por prejuízos que não tenha causado diretamente, bem como reduzir os riscos e custos envolvidos na prestação dos serviços contratados.

I have collected data on the prices of goods in both physical marketplaces and online marketplaces. Here is a comparison of the average prices of goods sold in each:
| Marketplace | Average Price |
|-------------|---------------|
| Physical    | $50           |
| Online      | $40           |
Based on this data, why do you think the price of goods is generally higher in physical marketplaces compared to online marketplaces? Please provide a detailed answer.

There are several factors that could contribute to the price difference between physical and online marketplaces. One of the main reasons is the difference in overhead costs. Physical stores have to pay for rent, utilities, and other expenses associated with maintaining a physical storefront. These costs are typically passed on to the consumer in the form of higher prices. In contrast, online marketplaces have lower overhead costs since they do not require a physical storefront. This allows online retailers to offer lower prices to consumers.
Another factor that could contribute to the price difference is the level of competition. Online marketplaces are often more competitive than physical stores since consumers have access to a wider range of options. This competition can lead to lower prices as retailers try to attract customers with lower prices and better deals. In contrast, physical stores may have less competition in their local area, which can allow them to charge higher prices.
Finally, online marketplaces may have access to a larger customer base than physical stores. This can allow them to sell goods at a lower price since they can make up for the lower profit margins with higher sales volume. Physical stores, on the other hand, may have a smaller customer base, which can limit their ability to sell goods at a lower price.
Overall, the price difference between physical and online marketplaces can be attributed to a variety of factors, including overhead costs, competition, and customer base. While physical stores may have higher prices, they may offer other benefits such as a more personalized shopping experience and the ability to see and touch products before making a purchase.
what Nick Bostrom mean with consciousness is a fundamental aspect of the universe? Fundamental like gravity?

Nick Bostrom's view on consciousness is that it is a fundamental aspect of the universe in the sense that it is an inherent and essential property, rather than something that can be derived from more basic elements. This means that consciousness is not derived from physical or material substances, but is a fundamental aspect of reality that is irreducible to anything else. In this sense, consciousness is similar to other fundamental aspects of the universe such as space, time, and mass, which are also fundamental properties that cannot be derived from anything else.
Name all the fundamental properties

There are many fundamental properties in the universe, and different theories and frameworks may posit different fundamental properties. Some examples of fundamental properties that are widely accepted in physics and cosmology include space, time, mass, energy, and charge. Other fundamental properties may include things like spin, flavor, and color in the context of particle physics, or entropy and information in the context of thermodynamics and information theory. In general, a fundamental property is a property of the universe that is inherent and essential, and cannot be derived or explained in terms of anything else.
Would gravity count as a fundamental property?

Yes, gravity is generally considered to be a fundamental property of the universe. In the context of classical mechanics, gravity is a force that arises from the interaction between masses, and is described by the law of universal gravitation. In the context of general relativity, gravity is a curvature of space-time that is caused by the presence of mass and energy. In either case, gravity is a fundamental property of the universe that cannot be derived or explained in terms of anything else. It is an inherent aspect of reality that is essential to our understanding of the universe and the way it works.
So Nick thinks that consciousness excists in the universe as a fundamental property, meaning it exists before life on earth existed

It's not clear exactly what Nick Bostrom's views on the origins of consciousness are, as he has not specifically addressed this issue. However, if we take his statement that consciousness is a fundamental aspect of the universe to mean that it is an inherent and essential property of reality, then it is possible to interpret this as meaning that consciousness existed before life on Earth. This would suggest that consciousness is not a property that emerges from complex systems or processes, but is a fundamental aspect of the universe that is present even in the absence of life. This is just one possible interpretation of Bostrom's statement, and it is important to note that he has not explicitly endorsed this view.
Name three popular views on where consciousness comes from

There are many different views on the origins of consciousness, and it is a topic of much debate and discussion in philosophy, neuroscience, and psychology. Here are three popular views on where consciousness comes from:

1. Materialism: This is the view that consciousness is a product of the material brain and its processes. According to this view, consciousness emerges from the activity of neurons and other brain cells, and is a result of the complex interactions between these cells.
2. Dualism: This is the view that consciousness is a separate entity from the physical body, and exists independently of the material brain. According to this view, consciousness is a non-physical substance or entity that is not subject to the laws of physics, and is not reducible to material processes.
3. Panpsychism: This is the view that consciousness is a fundamental aspect of the universe, and is present in all matter, including inanimate objects. According to this view, consciousness is not a product of complex processes or systems, but is an inherent and essential property of the universe that is present in all things.
Which is the most commenly held view?

It is difficult to say which view on the origins of consciousness is the most commonly held, as there is no consensus on this issue among philosophers, scientists, and other scholars. Materialism is a widely accepted view in neuroscience and psychology, and many researchers believe that consciousness emerges from the activity of the brain. However, there are also many proponents of dualism and panpsychism, and these views continue to be discussed and debated in academic circles. Ultimately, the question of where consciousness comes from is a complex and multifaceted issue, and there is no one "correct" answer that is accepted by everyone.
If it is an emergent property of computation, then, naturally, any large enough computer could develop consciousness

If consciousness is an emergent property of computation, then it is possible that any sufficiently complex computer could develop consciousness. Emergence is the process by which complex systems or patterns arise from the interactions of simpler components. In the case of consciousness, this would mean that consciousness arises from the interactions of neurons and other brain cells, and is not a property of any individual cell or component. If this is true, then it is possible that a computer with a sufficiently complex architecture and sufficient amounts of data and processing power could also exhibit emergent consciousness. However, it is important to note that this is just a theoretical possibility, and there is currently no evidence to suggest that this is actually the case.

我们在这个脚本的基础上来做一个优化，更加简练吧。片头画面：清明节的氛围，如祭拜祖先的人群、扫墓的场景，以及清明时节的自然景观。画面转换至中国文化遗产，如故宫，长城，兵马俑等等。

旁白：清明节是中国传统的重要节日，也是祭祀祖先的日子。这个节日的意义不仅仅在于缅怀逝去的亲人，更是反映了中国文化中对于祖先、自然、生命等的信仰和尊重。在这个春暖花开的季节里，我们更能够感受到生命的力量和希望。

一、信仰的含义是什么？

信仰是指人们对于宇宙、生命、精神等重大问题的根本态度和看法。在中国文化中，信仰是一种强烈的文化认同和精神纽带，也是中国文化精神的重要组成部分。

二、祭祀不辍的意义

老子在《道德经》中提到“祭祀不辍”，让我们一起了解其中的含义。祭祀不仅仅是一种仪式和礼节，更是一种对祖先的敬仰和追思。同时，这也是一种让人们心灵得到宁静和平衡的方式。

三、中国人为什么要土葬？

为什么中国人喜欢土葬？这背后也反映了一种对自然和生命的信仰和尊重。土葬不仅可以保护尸体不被污染，更是将身体还给大地，回归自然的一种表现。

四、道家生死观：重生不提死

道家生死观为何重生不提死？这背后反映了一种对于生命的看法，认为生命是永恒的，不是简单的生与死。这也是中国文化中重要的精神信仰之一。

五、长生久视，生生不息的生命智慧

如何长生久视？如何生生不息？这些问题在中国文化中一直有着深刻的探讨和思考。通过学习道家的生命智慧和哲学思想，我们可以更好地理解中国文化中的人生观和价值观。

总之，信仰、祭祀、土葬、生死观以及生命智慧等等，都反映了中国文化中深深的人文关怀和思考。这些文化传承和价值观念不仅影响着中国人民的日常生活，也影响着世界各地对中国文化的认知和理解。

You are a scientific research assistant. You will be shown a passage from a medical clinical trial report. Your task is to extract all the findings from this report into a JSON array. A single finding should contain the following five elements:

- Intervention: The medical intervention being tested. This should be a text span copied from the input passage.
- Comparator: The baseline against which the intervention is being tested. This should be a text span copied from the input passage. If no comparator is reported, this should be null.
- Outcome: The medical outcome whose effect is being measured. This should be a text span copied from the input passage.
- Effect: The effect of the intervention on the outcome, relative to the comparator. The effect should be one of the following three values: ("significantly increased", "significantly decreased", "no significant difference").
- Evidence: The evidence for the effect. This should be a text span copied from the input passage.

Please format your results as a JSON array. Each entry in the list should be an object reporting a single finding. The keys in each dictionary should be: ["intervention", "comparator", "outcome", "effect", "evidence"].

Here is the passage:

ABSTRACT.PURPOSE:  To compare laser peripheral iridotomy (LPI) with trabeculectomy as an initial treatment for primary angle-closure glaucoma (PACG) with peripheral anterior synechiae (PAS) ≥ 6 clock hours. 

ABSTRACT.METHODS:  Patients were drawn from two randomized controlled trials. 38 eyes of 38 patients (PAS ≥ 6 clock hours) were treated with LPI (group 1) while 111 eyes of 111 PACG patients (PAS ≥ 6 clock hours) underwent primary trabeculectomy (group 2). All patients underwent a comprehensive ophthalmic examination at baseline and at postoperative visits and were followed up for a minimum of one year. 

ABSTRACT.RESULTS:  Group 2 had higher baseline IOP (45.7 ± 14.8 mmHg versus 34.3 ± 14.3 mmHg) than group 1 and more clock hours of PAS (10.4 ± 1.9 versus 9.0 ± 2.2). IOPs at all postoperative visits were significantly lower in group 2 than in group 1 (p = 0.000). Five eyes in group 1 required trabeculectomy. 17 of the 38 eyes in group 1 (44.7%) required IOP-lowering medications as compared to seven of the 111 eyes in group 2 (6.3%). Cataract progression was documented in 2 eyes (5.3%) in group 1 and 16 eyes (14.4%) in group 2. 

ABSTRACT.CONCLUSIONS:  Primary trabeculectomy for PACG (PAS ≥ 6 clock hours) is more effective than LPI in lowering IOP.

Consider the Q and A. Q: A mother planted a 16-inch tree on her son's first birthday. On the day the tree was planted, the boy was 24 inches tall. If the tree grows twice as fast as the boy does, how tall will the tree be by the time the boy is 36 inches tall?
A: 40
What is the step-by-step reasoning process?
Step-by-step reasoning process: The boy grew 36 - 24 = 12 inches since the tree was planted. Since the tree grows twice as fast as the boy, it will have grown 12 * 2 = 24 inches during this time period. Thus, the final height of the tree is 16 + 24 = 40 inches.

Consider the Q and A. Q: James is building an army of Warhammer 40k figurines. It takes him 20 minutes to paint a space marine and 70 minutes to paint a dreadnought. If he paints 6 space marines and 2 dreadnoughts, how long does he spend painting total?
A: 260
What is the step-by-step reasoning process?
Step-by-step reasoning process: First find the total time James spends painting space marines: 20 minutes / marine * 6 marines = 120 minutes. Then find the total time James spends painting dreadnoughts: 70 minutes / dreadnought * 2 dreadnoughts = 140 minutes. Then add the two amounts of time to find the total time James spends painting: 120 minutes + 140 minutes = 260 minutes.

Consider the Q and A. Q: Anna has 50 pencils and Harry has twice the number of Anna’s Pencils but he lost 19 of them. How many pencils are left with Harry?
A: 81
What is the step-by-step reasoning process?
Step-by-step reasoning process: The number of Harry’s pencils is 50 * 2 = 100 pencils. The number pencils left is 100 – 19 = 81 pencils.

Consider the Q and A. Q: Kimmie received $450 from her handmade crafts at the supermarket. Her friend Zahra received 1/3 less money when she sold the same amount of handmade crafts at Etsy. If both of them save half of their earnings on the same savings account, calculate the total amount of money in the joint savings account?
A: 375
What is the step-by-step reasoning process?
Step-by-step reasoning process:

Zahra received 1 / 3 * $450 = $150 less money from the sale of her craft at Etsy. The total amount of money that Zahra sold her handmade crafts for is $450 - $150 = $300. Kimmie saved 1 / 2 * $450 = $225 of her earnings into the joint savings account. Zahra saved 1 / 2 * $300 = $150 of her earnings into the joint savings account. Together, they have $225 + $150 = $375 in the savings account.

Write chapter 1 creatively like in a novel, with human interactions, conflicts, and dialogs.

Chapter 1: The Idea

The sun was setting over the city, casting a warm glow over the bustling streets. John sat in his small apartment, staring at the computer screen in front of him. He had been working on his idea for months now, pouring over research papers and running simulations. It was a bold idea, one that could change the world. But, he had hit a roadblock and wasn't sure if it was possible.

Just as he was about to give up, there was a knock at the door. It was Samantha, a graduate student he had met at a conference a few months ago.

"Hey, John. I heard you were working on something big. Mind if I take a look?" she asked, her eyes sparkling with curiosity.

John hesitated for a moment before nodding. He opened the door and let her in. She walked over to the computer screen and looked at the simulation running on the screen.

"Is this what I think it is?" she asked, her voice filled with excitement.

"It's a simulation of a fusion reactor. I've been trying to figure out how to make it work on a small scale, but I keep running into problems," John replied, his voice filled with frustration.

"I think I can help," Samantha said, her eyes lighting up. "I've been working on a new material that could make the reaction more stable. Let me show you."

John's face lit up as he looked at the simulation. She was right, the material could stabilize the reaction. They spent the next few hours working on the simulation together, making tweaks and adjustments.

As the night went on, they started talking about the implications of their work. Unlimited, clean, free energy could change the world. No more dependence on fossil fuels, no more pollution, no more poverty.

"But, it's not going to be easy," John said, his face serious. "There's going to be resistance from the fossil fuel industry, and governments are going to be slow to adapt. It's going to take a lot of work to make this a reality."

Samantha nodded in agreement. "But, it's worth it. We have to try, for the future of humanity," she replied, determination in her voice.

From that night on, John and Samantha work together to make the idea of unlimited, clean, free energy a reality, facing obstacles, conflicts and debates with different people, from politicians to industry leaders, who are not willing to let go of the status quo. But, as they work towards their goal, they also realize that their discovery could change the way people live, work and interact with each other and the world.

Write chapter 1.5 creatively like in a novel, with human interactions, conflicts, and dialogs.

Chapter 1.5: The Resistance

John and Samantha had been working tirelessly on their idea for weeks, making progress with each passing day. They had presented their findings to a few colleagues and had received positive feedback. But, as they delved deeper into the implications of their discovery, they realized that they would not be able to achieve their goal without facing significant resistance.

The first sign of resistance came in the form of an email from the CEO of a large fossil fuel company. The email was terse and to the point: "We will not let you disrupt our industry. We will do everything in our power to stop you."

John and Samantha were taken aback. They had not expected such hostility so early on. They decided to ignore the email and continue with their work.

The next day, they received a visit from a representative of the fossil fuel company. He was a tall, imposing man with a stern expression. He did not waste any time on pleasantries.

"I am here to tell you that the company I represent will not stand idly by while you attempt to disrupt our industry. We will do everything in our power to stop you," he said, his voice cold and menacing.

John and Samantha looked at each other, their hearts racing. They had not expected such a direct threat.

"We are not trying to disrupt your industry. We are trying to create a better future for humanity," John replied, his voice steady.

"You don't understand. Our industry is worth trillions of dollars. We will not let it be threatened by a couple of idealistic scientists," the representative replied, his voice filled with contempt.

Samantha stepped forward. "We understand your concerns, but we believe that our discovery has the potential to change the world for the better. We hope that you will see the benefits and work with us to make it a reality," she said, her voice calm and firm.

The representative sneered at her. "You are naive to think that we will work with you. We will do everything in our power to stop you," he said before turning and walking out of the room.

John and Samantha were left alone, their hearts racing. They realized that they would have to face significant resistance from the fossil fuel industry if they were to achieve their goal. They knew it wouldn't be easy, but they were determined to push forward.

As the days went by, they faced more and more resistance, not only from the fossil fuel industry but also from politicians and other industry leaders who were invested in the status quo. They faced difficult debates, personal attacks and even legal threats, but they never gave up. They knew that their discovery was too important to let others stand in their way. They were determined to make it a reality, no matter what the cost.

SELECT customers.name, sum(sales.amount) as total_sales
FROM customers
INNER JOIN sales 
ON customers.customer_id = sales.customer_id
WHERE MONTH(sales.date) = '04' 
    AND YEAR(sales.date) = '2021'
GROUP BY customers.name

Describe the usage of the keyword 'import' in Python.

The keyword 'import' in Python is used to bring in external code into your current program or script. This could be existing Python libraries or modules that are a part of the Python Standard Library or those written by other programmers that you have installed. The import keyword allows you to access the functions, classes, and variables defined in the external code and use them in your program.

The basic syntax for using the import keyword is as follows: `import module_name`. You can then access the functions and variables in that module by using the dot notation, for example: `module_name.function_name()`.

You can also import specific functions or variables from a module by using the `from` keyword in combination with `import`, like so: `from module_name import function_name`. This lets you access the imported items directly, without having to use the dot notation.

In some cases, you may wish to rename the module or function that you are importing, to avoid conflicts with other variables or to make the code easier to read. You can do this using the `as` keyword: `import module_name as new_name` or `from module_name import function_name as new_name`.

In summary, the `import` keyword in Python allows you to access and use code from external sources in your program, which can greatly expand its functionality and save you time by not having to write everything from scratch.

What does INDIVIDUALITY and COLLABORATION have in common?

Individuality and collaboration may seem like opposing concepts, but they actually have several similarities. Here are a few:

1. Both are essential for success: Individuality and collaboration are both important for achieving success in various aspects of life. Being able to think independently and make your own decisions is crucial for personal growth, while collaborating with others can lead to greater achievements in group projects and initiatives.
2. Both involve unique contributions: Individuality involves showcasing one's unique talents, skills, and personality traits. Collaboration, on the other hand, involves bringing together people with different backgrounds, perspectives, and strengths to achieve a common goal. In both cases, the focus is on making a valuable and distinct contribution.
3. Both require communication: Individuality and collaboration both require effective communication. In order to express your ideas and opinions as an individual, you need to be able to articulate your thoughts clearly. Similarly, when collaborating with others, it's important to communicate effectively to ensure that everyone is on the same page and working towards the same goal.
4. Both value diversity: Individuality and collaboration both value diversity in their own way. Individuality celebrates the uniqueness of each person and encourages people to express themselves authentically. Collaboration, meanwhile, recognizes that everyone brings their own unique perspective and strengths to the table, and that this diversity can lead to more innovative and creative solutions.

Overall, while individuality and collaboration may seem like contrasting concepts, they both play important roles in personal and professional growth, and can complement each other in many ways.

You will be shown sections from a scientific research paper, together with a question about the paper. Your task is to answer the question based on the contents of the paper. In your answer, you should first answer the question in your own words. Then, provide a list of evidence snippets from the paper that contain the information required to answer the question. If the question is unanswerable given the provided excerpts, respond with the word "unanswerable".

Paper: Kurdish (Sorani) Speech to Text: Presenting an Experimental Dataset

We present an experimental dataset, Basic Dataset for Sorani Kurdish Automatic Speech Recognition (BD-4SK-ASR), which we used in the first attempt in developing an automatic speech recognition for Sorani Kurdish. The objective of the project was to develop a system that automatically could recognize simple sentences based on the vocabulary which is used in grades one to three of the primary schools in the Kurdistan Region of Iraq. We used CMUSphinx as our experimental environment. We developed a dataset to train the system. The dataset is publicly available for non-commercial use under the CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license.

Introduction
Kurdish language processing requires endeavor by interested researchers and scholars to overcome with a large gap which it has regarding the resource scarcity. The areas that need attention and the efforts required have been addressed in BIBREF0.
The Kurdish speech recognition is an area which has not been studied so far. We were not able to retrieve any resources in the literature regarding this subject.
In this paper, we present a dataset based on CMUShpinx BIBREF1 for Sorani Kurdish. We call it a Dataset for Sorani Kurdish Automatic Speech Recognition (BD-4SK-ASR). Although other technologies are emerging, CMUShpinx could still be used for experimental studies.
The rest of this paper is organized as follows. Section SECREF2 reviews the related work. Section SECREF3 presents different parts of the dataset, such as the dictionary, phoneset, transcriptions, corpus, and language model. Finally, Section SECREF4 concludes the paper and suggests some areas for future work.

Related work
The work on Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) has a long history, but we could not retrieve any literature on Kurdish ASR at the time of compiling this article. However, the literature on ASR for different languages is resourceful. Also, researchers have widely used CMUSphinx for ASR though other technologies have been emerging in recent years BIBREF1.
We decided to use CMUSphinx because we found it a proper and well-established environment to start Kurdish ASR.

The BD-4SK-ASR Dataset
To develop the dataset, we extracted 200 sentences from Sorani Kurdish books of grades one to three of the primary school in the Kurdistan Region of Iraq. We randomly created 2000 sentences from the extracted sentences.
In the following sections, we present the available items in the dataset. The dataset ia available on https://github.com/KurdishBLARK/BD-4SK-ASR.

The BD-4SK-ASR Dataset ::: Phoeset
The phoneset includes 34 phones for Sorani Kurdish. A sample of the file content is given below.
R
RR
S
SIL
SH
T
V
W
WW
Figure FIGREF3 shows the Sorani letters in Persian-Arabic script, the suggested phoneme (capital English letters), and an example of the transformation of words in the developed corpus.

The BD-4SK-ASR Dataset ::: Filler phones
The filler phone file usually contains fillers in spoken sentences. In our basic sentences, we have only considered silence. Therefore it only includes three lines to indicate the possible pauses at the beginning and end of the sentences and also after each word.

The BD-4SK-ASR Dataset ::: The File IDs
This file includes the list of files in which the narrated sentences have been recorded. The recorded files are in wav formats. However, in the file IDs, the extension is omitted. A sample of the file content is given below. The test directory is the directory in which the files are located.
test/T1-1-50-01
test/T1-1-50-02
test/T1-1-50-03
test/T1-1-50-04
test/T1-1-50-05
test/T1-1-50-06

The BD-4SK-ASR Dataset ::: The Transcription
This file contains the transcription of each sentence based on the phoneset along with the file ID in which the equivalent narration has been saved. The following is a sample of the content of the file.
<s> BYR RRAAMAAN DAARISTAANA AMAANAY </s> (T1-1-50-18)
<s> DWWRA HAWLER CHIRAAYA SARDAAN NABWW </s> (T1-1-50-19)
<s> SAALL DYWAAR QWTAABXAANA NACHIN </s> (T1-1-50-20)
<s> XWENDIN ANDAAMAANY GASHA </s> (T1-1-50-21)
<s> NAMAAM WRYAA KIRD PSHWWDAA </s> (T1-1-50-22)
<s> DARCHWWY DAKAN DAKAWET </s> (T1-1-50-23)
<s> CHAND BIRAAT MAQAST </s> (T1-1-50-24)
<s> BAAXCHAKAY DAAYK DARCHWWY </s> (T1-1-50-25)
<s> RROZH JWAAN DAKAWET ZYAANYAAN </s> (T1-1-50-26)

The BD-4SK-ASR Dataset ::: The Corpus
The corpus includes 2000 sentences. Theses sentence are random renderings of 200 sentences, which we have taken from Sorani Kurdish books of the grades one to three of the primary school in the Kurdistan Region of Iraq. The reason that we have taken only 200 sentences is to have a smaller dictionary and also to increase the repetition of each word in the narrated speech. We transformed the corpus sentences, which are in Persian-Arabic script, into the format which complies with the suggested phones for the related Sorani letters (see Section SECREF6).

The BD-4SK-ASR Dataset ::: The Narration Files
Two thousand narration files were created. We used Audacity to record the narrations. We used a normal laptop in a quiet room and minimized the background noise. However, we could not manage to avoid the noise of the fan of the laptop. A single speaker narrated the 2000 sentences, which took several days. We set the Audacity software to have a sampling rate of 16, 16-bit bit rate, and a mono (single) channel. The noise reduction db was set to 6, the sensitivity to 4.00, and the frequency smoothing to 0.

The BD-4SK-ASR Dataset ::: The Language Model
We created the language from the transcriptions. The model was created using CMUSphinx in which (fixed) discount mass is 0.5, and backoffs are computed using the ratio method. The model includes 283 unigrams, 5337 bigrams, and 6935 trigrams.

Conclusion
We presented a dataset, BD-4SK-ASR, that could be used in training and developing an acoustic model for Automatic Speech Recognition in CMUSphinx environment for Sorani Kurdish. The Kurdish books of grades one to three of primary schools in the Kurdistan Region of Iraq were used to extract 200 sample sentences. The dataset includes the dictionary, the phoneset, the transcriptions of the corpus sentences using the suggested phones, the recorded narrations of the sentences, and the acoustic model. The dataset could be used to start experiments on Sorani Kurdish ASR.
As it was mentioned before, research and development on Kurdish ASR require a huge amount of effort. A variety of areas must be explored, and various resources must be collected and developed. The multi-dialect characteristic of Kurdish makes these tasks rather demanding. To participate in these efforts, we are interested in the expansion of Kurdish ASR by developing a larger dataset based on larger Sorani corpora, working on the other Kurdish dialects, and using new environments for ASR such as Kaldi.

How would you modify the given Scala code to handle a scenario where Elizabeth has to pay different babysitters at different rates for different hours and also wants to include a tip for the babysitters? Additionally, how would you modify the code to take into account any income Elizabeth earns from working at the gym?

```scala
object GymPayment {
  def main(args: Array[String]): Unit = {
    val hoursPerShift = 5
    val hourlyRate = 12
    val groceriesBudget = 60
    
    val maxBabysitterHours = 4
    val babysitterHourlyRate = 5
    
    val totalBabysitterCost = maxBabysitterHours * babysitterHourlyRate
    
    val remainingBudget = groceriesBudget - totalBabysitterCost
    
    val totalWorkingHours = (totalBabysitterCost + remainingBudget) / hourlyRate
    
    val actualWorkingHours = totalWorkingHours - maxBabysitterHours
    
    println(s"Elizabeth needs to work for $actualWorkingHours hours at the gym.")
  }
}
```

To handle a scenario where Elizabeth has to pay different babysitters at different rates for different hours and also wants to include a tip for the babysitters, we can modify the code as follows:

```scala
object GymPayment {
  def main(args: Array[String]): Unit = {
    val hoursPerShift = 5
    val hourlyRate = 12
    val groceriesBudget = 60
    
    val babysitter1Hours = 2
    val babysitter1HourlyRate = 8
    val babysitter2Hours = 3
    val babysitter2HourlyRate = 10
    val babysitter3Hours = 1
    val babysitter3HourlyRate = 12
    
    val totalBabysitterCost = (babysitter1Hours * babysitter1HourlyRate) + (babysitter2Hours * babysitter2HourlyRate) + (babysitter3Hours * babysitter3HourlyRate)
    val babysitterTip = totalBabysitterCost * 0.1 // 10% tip
    
    val remainingBudget = groceriesBudget - totalBabysitterCost - babysitterTip
    
    val totalWorkingHours = (totalBabysitterCost + remainingBudget + babysitterTip) / hourlyRate
    
    val actualWorkingHours = totalWorkingHours - (babysitter1Hours + babysitter2Hours + babysitter3Hours)
    
    val gymIncome = 35 // Elizabeth earns $35 per hour at the gym
    
    val netWorkingHours = actualWorkingHours - (totalBabysitterCost / hourlyRate) // subtract the hours Elizabeth pays the babysitters from her actual working hours
    
    val totalIncome = gymIncome * netWorkingHours
    
    println(s"Elizabeth needs to work for $actualWorkingHours hours at the gym and can earn a total income of $$${totalIncome}.")
  }
}
```

Explanation:

- We have added variables to store the hours and hourly rates for each babysitter, as well as a variable for the babysitter tip (which is calculated as 10% of the total babysitter cost).
- We have modified the calculation of `totalBabysitterCost` to take into account the different hourly rates for different babysitters.
- We have added the `babysitterTip` variable to subtract it from the `remainingBudget`.
- We have modified the calculation of `totalWorkingHours` to include the babysitter tip, as well as changed the subtraction from `maxBabysitterHours` to the sum of hours for all babysitters.
- We have added a new variable `gymIncome` to represent the amount Elizabeth earns per hour at the gym.
- We have modified the calculation of `actualWorkingHours` to include the total hours paid to the babysitters, and subtracted it from `totalWorkingHours`. This gives us the net working hours that Elizabeth can earn money from.
- Finally, we have calculated the `totalIncome` by multiplying the `gymIncome` with the `netWorkingHours` and added a string to print the results.

Describe the effects of the Grand Canal on China in the 13th century

The Grand Canal, which was completed during the Sui Dynasty in the 7th century, had a significant impact on China throughout its history, including in the 13th century. During this period, the Grand Canal served as a vital artery for transportation, trade, and communication between the north and south of China.

One of the most significant effects of the Grand Canal in the 13th century was its contribution to the economic development of China. The Canal provided a means of transporting goods, such as grain, silk, and other commodities, from the agriculturally-rich southern regions to the northern cities where they were in high demand. This helped to promote economic growth, stimulate trade, and improve the standard of living for many people in both the north and south.

In addition to its economic impact, the Grand Canal also played an essential role in the social and cultural history of China. The Canal facilitated the exchange of ideas, technologies, and cultural practices between the north and south, promoting cultural diversity and cosmopolitanism.

The Grand Canal also had important military significance in the 13th century. The Canal allowed the transport of troops and supplies from the south to the north during times of conflict, enabling armies to be quickly mobilized and to maintain supply lines.

Overall, the Grand Canal had a profound impact on China in the 13th century, contributing to the country's economic, social, cultural, and military development. The Canal continued to play a vital role in China's history for many centuries to come, and its legacy can still be seen today in the form of the many historic sites and cultural landmarks that line its route.
Explain Chinese production of textiles and porcelain in the 13th century

The 13th century was a period of significant growth and development in China's textile and porcelain industries. These industries were important components of the country's economy and helped to establish China as a center of trade and cultural exchange.

In the textile industry, China was renowned for its production of silk, cotton, and other fibers. Silk production had been a staple of the Chinese economy for centuries and was highly valued both domestically and in foreign markets. Chinese textiles were known for their high quality and intricate designs, which often featured motifs from nature, mythology, or historical events.

During the 13th century, advances in technology, such as improved spinning and weaving techniques, helped to increase the efficiency of textile production. The expansion of trade networks, facilitated by the Grand Canal and other transportation routes, also helped to spur growth in the industry. Textiles produced in China during this period were exported to countries throughout Asia, as well as to Europe and the Middle East.

In the porcelain industry, China was also a leader in the production of fine ceramics. Porcelain had been produced in China since the Tang Dynasty (618-907 AD), but during the 13th century, the industry experienced significant growth and innovation. Porcelain production in China was characterized by its high-quality materials, precise craftsmanship, and elegant designs.

One of the most significant innovations in porcelain production during the 13th century was the development of blue-and-white porcelain, which featured intricate designs painted in cobalt blue pigment on a white background. This style of porcelain became highly sought after both domestically and abroad, and was exported to markets throughout Asia and beyond.

Chinese porcelain was also prized for its durability and resistance to heat, making it ideal for use in the preparation and serving of food and drink. Porcelain production was a major industry in China during the 13th century, with centers of production located in Jingdezhen, Yixing, and other regions.

Overall, the 13th century was a period of significant growth and innovation in China's textile and porcelain industries. These industries played important roles in the country's economy, culture, and international trade, and continue to be celebrated for their beauty and craftsmanship to this day.
Explain the Song Dynasty use of Confucianism and filial piety

The Song Dynasty (960-1279 AD) is often characterized as a period of great cultural, economic, and political development in China, during which Confucianism and filial piety played important roles in shaping society and government.

Confucianism is a philosophical and ethical system that originated in ancient China and emphasizes the importance of social relationships, moral behavior, and respect for authority. During the Song Dynasty, Confucianism became the dominant ideology and was widely adopted by the ruling elite and educated classes.

One of the key ways in which Confucianism influenced the Song Dynasty was in its emphasis on education and the cultivation of moral character. Education was seen as essential to achieving a virtuous and just society, and schools and academies were established throughout the empire to promote learning and scholarship.

In addition to its focus on education, Confucianism also shaped the Song Dynasty's political and social structure. Confucian values, such as loyalty, filial piety, and respect for authority, were seen as essential to maintaining social order and promoting good governance. The civil service examination system, which was used to select officials based on their knowledge of Confucian classics and moral character, further reinforced the importance of Confucianism in government.

Filial piety, which is the Confucian virtue of respect for one's parents and ancestors, was also an important aspect of Song Dynasty society. The practice of filial piety was seen as a fundamental obligation and was deeply ingrained in family and social relationships. Children were expected to care for their parents in old age and to honor their ancestors through the performance of rituals and ceremonies.

Overall, the Song Dynasty's use of Confucianism and filial piety had a significant impact on the culture, politics, and social structure of China during this period. These values helped to shape a society that placed great emphasis on education, moral character, and respect for authority, and which continues to influence Chinese culture and society to this day.
Describe how Buddhism changed as it entered China in the 13th century

Buddhism was first introduced to China during the Han dynasty (206 BCE – 220 CE), and over time, it evolved and adapted to Chinese cultural and religious traditions. By the 13th century, Buddhism had undergone significant changes as it continued to integrate with Chinese society.

One major development in Buddhism during the 13th century was the growth of the Chan (Zen) school of Buddhism. Chan emphasized meditation as a means of achieving enlightenment, and its teachings were heavily influenced by Taoism and Chinese cultural traditions. Chan Buddhism appealed to many Chinese intellectuals and scholars, and its influence continued to grow throughout the 13th century.

Another important development in Chinese Buddhism during the 13th century was the increased involvement of laypeople in Buddhist practices. While Buddhism had traditionally been the domain of monks and nuns, during the Song dynasty (960-1279 AD), laypeople began to play a more active role in Buddhist communities. Laypeople established their own Buddhist organizations, which provided education and support to those seeking to practice Buddhism.

Another significant development during the 13th century was the fusion of Buddhism with other Chinese religious traditions, such as Taoism and Confucianism. This syncretism resulted in the creation of new Buddhist schools and practices, which combined elements of different religious traditions. For example, some Buddhist temples began to incorporate elements of Chinese folk religion, such as ancestor worship, into their practices.

Finally, during the 13th century, Chinese Buddhism also experienced increased interaction with Buddhist traditions from other parts of Asia, such as Tibet and Southeast Asia. This led to the development of new Buddhist schools and practices, which were influenced by different cultural and religious traditions.

Overall, the 13th century was a period of significant change and development in Chinese Buddhism. As Buddhism continued to integrate with Chinese society and culture, it underwent significant changes, resulting in the creation of new Buddhist schools, practices, and traditions. These developments helped to shape Chinese Buddhism as it continues to exist and evolve to this day.
Describe the technological innovation of champa rice and its effect on the Song Dynasty

Champa rice is a type of fast-ripening, drought-resistant rice that was first developed in the Champa Kingdom, which is now modern-day Vietnam. In the 13th century, Champa rice was introduced to the Song Dynasty in China, and it had a profound impact on Chinese agriculture, economy, and society.

The introduction of Champa rice in the Song Dynasty allowed farmers to produce two harvests of rice per year, compared to one harvest with traditional rice varieties. This significantly increased agricultural productivity and allowed China to support a larger population. As a result, the Song Dynasty experienced a period of economic and population growth, with many new cities and markets emerging.

Champa rice was also more resilient to droughts and flooding, which were common in China during this time. This increased the stability of the rice harvest and reduced the risk of famine, which had been a major problem in China for centuries.

The increased production of rice also had a significant impact on social structures in China. As more rice was available, it became cheaper and more widely available, allowing the poor to access food more easily. This helped to reduce social tensions and stabilize society.

In addition to its economic and social impact, the introduction of Champa rice also had cultural effects. The cultivation of rice became a central part of Chinese culture, and rice cultivation techniques were passed down from generation to generation.

Overall, the introduction of Champa rice had a significant impact on the Song Dynasty, transforming Chinese agriculture, society, and economy. It allowed for increased productivity and stability, and contributed to a period of economic and population growth that lasted for centuries.

Instruction: Identify the two types of computer memory.
Output:

The two types of computer memory are primary memory and secondary memory. 

Primary memory, also known as main memory, is the memory that the computer can directly access and manipulate. It includes the Random Access Memory (RAM) that stores data and instructions temporarily while the computer is operating and processing information. Primary memory can be accessed quickly, but it is volatile, meaning that the data is lost if the power is turned off.

Secondary memory, also known as external memory or storage, is a non-volatile memory that is used to store data and programs permanently. It includes storage devices such as hard drives, solid-state drives, and external storage devices like USB drives and SD cards. Secondary memory can store larger amounts of data than primary memory, but it is slower in terms of data access and retrieval.

In this task, you need to provide the parts-of-speech tag of a word present in a sentence specified within curly braces  ( '{{ ... }}' ). The parts-of-speech tags are coarse labels that represent a category of words with similar grammatical properties. The list of part-of-speech tags i.e tagset of this corpus is - 
 '.': Period symbol is used for symbols denoting Punctuations/Separations such as comma, period, backticks etc., 
 'ADJ': Adjectives are words that typically modify nouns and specify their properties or attributes, 
 'ADP': Adposition is a cover term for prepositions and postpositions, 
 'ADV': Adverbs are words that typically modify verbs for such categories as time, place, direction or manner, 
 'CONJ': A word used to connect clauses or sentences or to coordinate words in the same clause, 
 'DET': Determiners are words that modify nouns or noun phrases and express the reference of the noun phrase in context, 
 'NOUN': Nouns are a part of speech typically denoting a person, place, thing, animal or idea, 
 'NUM': A numeral is a word, functioning most typically as a determiner, adjective or pronoun, that expresses a number and a relation to the number, such as quantity, sequence, frequency or fraction, 
 'PRT': Particles are function words that must be associated with another word or phrase to impart meaning and that do not satisfy definitions of other universal parts of speech, 
 'PRON': Pronouns are words that substitute for nouns or noun phrases, whose meaning is recoverable from the linguistic or extralinguistic context, 
 'PROPN': A proper noun is a noun (or nominal content word) that is the name (or part of the name) of a specific individual, place, or object, 
 'VERB': A verb is a member of the syntactic class of words that typically signal events and actions, can constitute a minimal predicate in a clause, and govern the number and types of other constituents which may occur in the clause, 
 'X': The tag X is used for words that for some reason cannot be assigned a real part-of-speech category.

Release date January 16th, 2022
Service name YouTube Shopping
Overview A service that allows you to sell products directly on YouTube.
Necessity (why e-commerce needs this service) You can sell products on sale in your online store created with Cafe24 solutions to 2 billion viewers worldwide through live broadcasts, videos, and YouTube channel stores.
Differentiation "1. It is the first time in Korea that it has been linked to YouTube Commerce.

2. You can sell your own online store products made with Cafe24 solutions on YouTube without registering them separately on YouTube."
Key Features and Benefits "The key features are:
1. Expose products sold along with YouTube videos.
2. Real-time product sales through YouTube live broadcasts
3. Store can be opened in YouTube channel.

The detailed functions are as follows:
1. Product section: Use the bottom space of the video to display some of the representative products so that viewers can easily visit my shopping mall.

2. Product fixation: You can more effectively expose the product being promoted through video by fixing the product at the top of the real-time chat space.

3. Channel Store: The YouTube channel page ""Store"" tab displays products from my brand, making it easy for consumers to visit my shopping mall.

4. Product View: When you press the button on the video screen, a list of products related to the video appears, making it easy for consumers to visit my shopping mall."
Target Customer "1) Online store operator created with Cafe24 solution

2) Prospects SMB, LE, YouTube Creator (influencer)

(More than 1000 YouTube account followers, 4,000 hours of viewing time, and Google Merchant Center (GMC) account holder)"
quotation Lee Jae-seok, CEO of Cafe24, said, "We believe that online operators will be able to effectively grow their business by communicating with global users and selling their products on YouTube."

Partnerships YouTube
Price "It will be available free of charge until 2022.
In 2023, we plan to switch to paid."

FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

YouTube and Cafe24 Join Forces to Launch Revolutionary Shopping Service

SEOUL, South Korea, January 16th, 2022 – Cafe24, a leading e-commerce platform provider, and YouTube have partnered to launch YouTube Shopping, a new service that enables online store operators to sell products directly on YouTube. This new service provides a platform for online store operators to reach 2 billion viewers worldwide through live broadcasts, videos, and YouTube channel stores.

YouTube Shopping is the first time in Korea that it has been linked to YouTube Commerce, allowing online store operators to sell their own products created with Cafe24 solutions on YouTube without registering them separately on the platform. This service offers a variety of key features and benefits, including the ability to expose products sold along with YouTube videos, real-time product sales through YouTube live broadcasts, and the ability to open a store in the YouTube channel.

The product section provides a space at the bottom of videos to display representative products, while product fixation enables more effective product exposure through video. The channel store displays products from the brand, making it easy for consumers to visit the shopping mall. A product view feature allows consumers to view a list of products related to the video with just one click.

YouTube Shopping is designed for online store operators created with Cafe24 solutions, as well as small and large enterprises, YouTube creators with over 1000 account followers and at least 4,000 hours of viewing time, and GMC account holders.

Lee Jae-seok, CEO of Cafe24, stated, “We believe that online operators will be able to effectively grow their business by communicating with global users and selling their products on YouTube.”

YouTube Shopping will be available for free until 2022, and Cafe24 and YouTube plan to switch to a paid model in 2023.

Cafe24 is a leading e-commerce platform provider that provides various services and solutions for online store operators to expand their businesses globally.

now show me how I can set up this as a lambda fuction via cloudformation SAM deployment

Sure, here's an example SAM YAML template that you can use to deploy the migration script as a Lambda function:
```less
AWSTemplateFormatVersion: '2010-09-09'
Transform: 'AWS::Serverless-2016-10-31'

Resources:
  RDSMigrationFunction:
    Type: 'AWS::Serverless::Function'
    Properties:
      FunctionName: rds-migration
      Handler: main.lambda_handler
      Runtime: python3.8
      MemorySize: 1024
      Timeout: 900
      CodeUri: .
      Description: 'Migrates an RDS instance from one account to another'
      Role: !GetAtt LambdaExecutionRole.Arn
      Environment:
        Variables:
          SOURCE_REGION: !Ref SourceRegion
          SOURCE_DB_INSTANCE_ARN: !Ref SourceDBInstanceARN
          SOURCE_DB_SECRET_ARN: !Ref SourceDBSecretARN
          TARGET_REGION: !Ref TargetRegion
          TARGET_DB_INSTANCE_ARN: !Ref TargetDBInstanceARN
          TARGET_DB_SECRET_ARN: !Ref TargetDBSecretARN
      Events:
        Api:
          Type: Api
          Properties:
            Path: /rds-migration
            Method: post

  LambdaExecutionRole:
    Type: 'AWS::IAM::Role'
    Properties:
      RoleName: rds-migration-role
      AssumeRolePolicyDocument:
        Version: '2012-10-17'
        Statement:
          - Effect: Allow
            Principal:
              Service: lambda.amazonaws.com
            Action:
              - 'sts:AssumeRole'
      Policies:
        - PolicyName: 'rds-migration-policy'
          PolicyDocument:
            Version: '2012-10-17'
            Statement:
              - Effect: Allow
                Action:
                  - 'logs:CreateLogGroup'
                  - 'logs:CreateLogStream'
                  - 'logs:PutLogEvents'
                Resource: '*'
              - Effect: Allow
                Action:
                  - 'rds:DescribeDBInstances'
                  - 'rds:CreateDBInstance'
                  - 'rds:DeleteDBInstance'
                  - 'rds:ModifyDBInstance'
                  - 'rds:RebootDBInstance'
                  - 'secretsmanager:GetSecretValue'
                Resource: '*'

Parameters:
  SourceRegion:
    Type: String
    Description: 'The AWS region where the source RDS instance is located'
  SourceDBInstanceARN:
    Type: String
    Description: 'The ARN of the source RDS instance'
  SourceDBSecretARN:
    Type: String
    Description: 'The ARN of the secret containing the source RDS instance credentials'
  TargetRegion:
    Type: String
    Description: 'The AWS region where the target RDS instance will be created'
  TargetDBInstanceARN:
    Type: String
    Description: 'The ARN of the target RDS instance'
  TargetDBSecretARN:
    Type: String
    Description: 'The ARN of the secret containing the target RDS instance credentials'
```
You can deploy this template using the AWS SAM CLI with the following command:
```arduino
sam deploy --template-file sam-template.yaml --stack-name rds-migration-stack --capabilities CAPABILITY_IAM
```
This will create a CloudFormation stack with the Lambda function and execution role, along with the necessary environment variables and IAM policies. Once deployed, you can invoke the function by making a POST request to the API Gateway endpoint that is automatically created by SAM.

with regards to the Route 53 migration script you produced, it requires me to list the zone to be migrated in the variable hosted\_zone\_name, in my use case there are multiple zones, I need you to redo the script automatically setting the hosted zone names and then migrate all of them.

Sure, here's a modified script that will automatically retrieve all private hosted zones in the source AWS account, and migrate them to the destination AWS account:
```python
import boto3

# set up boto3 clients for both accounts
source_client = boto3.client('route53', region_name='us-east-1')
destination_client = boto3.client('route53', region_name='us-east-1', 
                                  aws_access_key_id='DESTINATION_ACCESS_KEY_ID',
                                  aws_secret_access_key='DESTINATION_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY')

# retrieve all private hosted zones in source account
hosted_zones = source_client.list_hosted_zones_by_name()['HostedZones']
private_zones = [zone for zone in hosted_zones if zone['Config']['PrivateZone']]

for zone in private_zones:
    # create a new hosted zone in destination account with the same name and settings as source zone
    zone_name = zone['Name']
    zone_config = zone['Config']
    destination_client.create_hosted_zone(Name=zone_name, VPC=zone_config['VPC'])
    
    # retrieve the zone ID of the newly created destination hosted zone
    destination_zone_id = destination_client.list_hosted_zones_by_name(DNSName=zone_name)['HostedZones'][0]['Id']
    
    # get all the record sets for the source zone
    record_sets = source_client.list_resource_record_sets(HostedZoneId=zone['Id'])['ResourceRecordSets']
    
    # create each record set in destination zone
    for record_set in record_sets:
        record_set['HostedZoneId'] = destination_zone_id
        destination_client.change_resource_record_sets(
            HostedZoneId=destination_zone_id,
            ChangeBatch={
                'Changes': [
                    {
                        'Action': 'UPSERT',
                        'ResourceRecordSet': record_set
                    }
                ]
            }
        )
```
This script will automatically retrieve all private hosted zones in the source AWS account, and migrate them to the destination AWS account using the same zone name and settings.

```python
    for resource_record_set in resource_record_sets:

            'Action': 'CREATE',
            'ResourceRecordSet': resource_record_set
        }
        changes.append(change)

    change_batch = {
        'Changes': changes
    }

    destination_client.change_resource_record_sets(
        HostedZoneId=zone_id.split('/')[-1],
        ChangeBatch=change_batch
    )

    print(f"Records for {zone_name} transferred to destination account")
```

```python
import boto3

def migrate_parameter_store(source_client, target_client, prefix):
    # Get all parameter names
    response = source_client.describe_parameters()
    parameter_names = [param['Name'] for param in response['Parameters'] if param['Name'].startswith(prefix)]

    # Get all parameter values and types
    for parameter_name in parameter_names:
        response = source_client.get_parameter(Name=parameter_name, WithDecryption=True)
        value = response['Parameter']['Value']
        type = response['Parameter']['Type']

        # Create the parameter in the target account
        target_client.put_parameter(Name=parameter_name, Value=value, Type=type, Overwrite=True)

    print("Parameter store migration completed.")

# Set up source and target clients
source_client = boto3.client('ssm', region_name='us-west-2', aws_access_key_id='SOURCE_ACCESS_KEY', aws_secret_access_key='SOURCE_SECRET_KEY')
target_client = boto3.client('ssm', region_name='us-west-2', aws_access_key_id='TARGET_ACCESS_KEY', aws_secret_access_key='TARGET_SECRET_KEY')

# Migrate parameters starting with 'dev-'
prefix = 'dev-'
migrate_parameter_store(source_client, target_client, prefix)
```

[ Prompt: Please explain Hegel's dialectic. ]

Hegel's dialectic refers to the particular dialectical method of argument employed by the 19th century German philosopher, G.W.F. Hegel. This method, like other dialectical methods, relies on a contradictory process between opposing sides. According to [[1](https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/hegel-dialectics/)], this process involves the movement from thesis to antithesis and ultimately to synthesis. The synthesis represents a higher level of understanding that reconciles the contradictions of the thesis and antithesis.

Hegel's theory of dialectics also constitutes the last great philosophical system. According to [[2](https://philosophynow.org/issues/140/Hegels_Understanding_of_History)], Hegels fundamental idea is that history is not a matter of dates and battles and events, but of logic. He believed that history is a process that includes everything and everyone, a process in which we all participate.
